title: Modules as Namespaces 🏷️
description: Prevent class name collisions in massive applications.

slides.title: The Naming Collision 💥
slides.content:
Imagine you are building a giant Video Game in Ruby. You create a `class Physics`. 

Then, you decide to download a 3rd-party mapping library... and the creator of that library ALSO named one of their core classes `class Physics`.

Ruby instantly crashes! You cannot have two identical Classes in the same app.

slides.title: Namespaces to the Rescue 📦
slides.content:
To definitively solve this, Ruby allows you to perfectly wrap your Classes inside Modules!

This neatly creates an isolated "Namespace", beautifully ensuring that your `Physics` class is legally completely separate from their `Physics` class.

slides.title: Using the Scope Operator
slides.content: Run this! Notice the magnificent double-colon `::` symbol. This explicitly tells Ruby precisely where specifically inside the Module to locate the class.
slides.codeSnippet:
module MyGame
  class Physics
    def gravity; puts "Game gravity is 9.8"; end
  end
end

module ThirdPartyLibrary
  class Physics
    def gravity; puts "Mapping gravity is 0"; end
  end
end

# Safely instantiate explicitly both identical classes!
game_physics = MyGame::Physics.new
lib_physics = ThirdPartyLibrary::Physics.new

game_physics.gravity
lib_physics.gravity

quizData.question: Which specific incredibly powerful symbol tells Ruby explicitly to look INSIDE a specific Module for a Class?
quizData.explanation: The double-colon explicitly firmly neatly operates visually specifically purely strictly identically to seamlessly safely neatly navigating solidly quickly through efficiently carefully logically secure folders.

slides.title: Why Use Namespaces?
slides.content: By rigorously putting all your Custom Classes into correctly named Modules, you avoid namespace collision in larger apps. Think of modules here as folders that securely group your correctly named classes.
